evals.prompt: I have a React app with no i18n set up. The LoginForm component has hardcoded text: title 'Log in to your account', email label 'Email address', password label 'Password', and a submit button 'Sign in'. Set up react-i18next and replace all the hardcoded strings with translation keys. Support both English and Thai. Default to English.
evals.expected_output: i18n config file created, EN and TH locale JSON files with matching keys, LoginForm updated to use useTranslation and t(), and a LanguageSwitcher component provided.
assertions: Creates src/i18n.ts or equivalent config file that initialises react-i18next with fallbackLng set to 'en' and supportedLngs including both 'en' and 'th'
Creates a locale file for English (e.g. public/locales/en/translation.json) containing keys for all 4 hardcoded strings
Creates a matching locale file for Thai with the same keys and Thai translations
LoginForm no longer contains raw English string literals inside JSX — uses t('...') calls instead
Key parity: EN and TH files have identical key sets
Provides a LanguageSwitcher component or equivalent that can toggle between en and th

evals.prompt: My Vue 3 app uses Vite and has no i18n. There are hardcoded strings scattered across three components: NavBar.vue ('Dashboard', 'Settings', 'Logout'), DashboardPage.vue ('Welcome back!', 'Total Orders', 'Revenue'), and ProductForm.vue ('Add Product', 'Product Name', 'Price', 'Save', 'Cancel'). Set up vue-i18n with English and Thai support. Use flat dot-notation keys grouped by page/component. Default language should be English.
evals.expected_output: vue-i18n installed and configured, flat EN/TH JSON files with all keys, all three components updated to use useI18n and t(), language switcher component provided.
assertions: Creates src/i18n/index.ts or equivalent that initialises vue-i18n in Composition API (legacy: false) mode with locale defaulting to 'en'
Creates src/locales/en.json with flat dot-notation keys for all hardcoded strings from all three components
Creates src/locales/th.json with matching keys and Thai translations
NavBar.vue uses t() for all three nav items and imports useI18n
DashboardPage.vue and ProductForm.vue similarly replace all raw strings with t() calls
Key parity: en.json and th.json have the same set of keys
Common strings like Save and Cancel are placed under a common.* namespace and reused rather than duplicated

evals.prompt: We already have react-i18next set up in our Next.js (Pages Router) app. There are locale files at public/locales/en/translation.json and public/locales/th/translation.json. But the dev who built the profile settings page forgot about i18n — ProfileSettings.tsx has hardcoded: heading 'Profile Settings', name field label 'Full Name', email field label 'Email', bio textarea label 'Bio', a save button 'Save Changes', and a success toast 'Profile updated successfully!'. Audit the file, add the missing keys to both locale files, and update ProfileSettings.tsx to use t().
evals.expected_output: 6 new keys added to both EN and TH locale files, ProfileSettings.tsx updated to use useTranslation and t(), no hardcoded English strings remaining in the component.
assertions: Adds exactly the keys needed for the 6 hardcoded strings to public/locales/en/translation.json without removing existing keys
Adds matching Thai translations for all 6 new keys to public/locales/th/translation.json
ProfileSettings.tsx imports useTranslation and destructures t from it
All 6 previously hardcoded strings are replaced with t('...') calls
No new hardcoded English sentence strings remain in ProfileSettings.tsx
Key parity maintained: en and th files still have the same set of keys after additions
